text: High load strength training (≥80% 1RM) improves running economy in middle- and long-distance runners with a small effect size across speeds from 8.64 to 17.85 km/h.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Combined strength training methods (two or more methods together) produce moderate improvements in running economy across speeds from 10.00 to 14.45 km/h.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Plyometric training improves running economy only at speeds ≤12.00 km/h (≤7:27 min/mile), with no significant benefit at higher speeds.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Submaximal load strength training (40-79% 1RM) does not significantly improve running economy compared to endurance training alone.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Isometric strength training does not significantly improve running economy compared to endurance training alone.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: High load strength training produces greater running economy improvements in athletes with higher VO2max values.
population: middle- and long-distance runners with high VO2max

text: High load strength training produces greater running economy improvements at higher running speeds.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Effective strength training programs for improving running economy range from 6 to 24 weeks in duration with 1 to 4 sessions per week.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)
